eli3: Ito's lemma is the chain rule of stochastic calculus. In ordinary calculus, if y = f(x) and x changes by dx, then dy = f'(x) dx. But when x follows a random process (Brownian motion), there's an extra term: dy = f'(x) dx + ВҪ f''(x) (dx)ВІ. The second-order term survives because Brownian motion increments have (dW)ВІ = dt, not zero.

This extra term changes everything in finance. It's why the log of a stock price grows at Ој - ПғВІ/2, not Ој (a fact crucial for understanding long-term portfolio returns). It's how Black and Scholes derived their option pricing formula. It's the foundation of all continuous-time finance.

Intuition for (dW)ВІ = dt: the variance of a Brownian increment over time dt is exactly dt. In ordinary calculus, infinitesimal increments squared are negligible (dxВІ вҶ’ 0). But Brownian motion is so rough that its squared increments are not negligible вҖ” they accumulate to a finite quantity over time.

whenWhy.title: Applying Ito's lemma: from GBM to log-normal
whenWhy.body: Given S follows GBM: dS = ОјS dt + ПғS dW. Define f(S) = ln(S). By Ito's lemma: d(ln S) = (вҲӮln S/вҲӮS) dS + ВҪ (вҲӮВІln S/вҲӮSВІ)(dS)ВІ. Compute: вҲӮln S/вҲӮS = 1/S; вҲӮВІln S/вҲӮSВІ = -1/SВІ. Substitute: d(ln S) = (1/S)(ОјS dt + ПғS dW) + ВҪ(-1/SВІ)(ПғS)ВІ dt = Ој dt + Пғ dW - ПғВІ/2 dt = (Ој - ПғВІ/2) dt + Пғ dW. This is the log-return process: normally distributed with drift (Ој - ПғВІ/2) and diffusion Пғ. The -ПғВІ/2 correction (the Jensen/Ito correction) is critical вҖ” it means the median log-return is less than the mean return Ој. For Ој = 10%, Пғ = 20%: median annual return = 10% - 2% = 8%. The -ПғВІ/2 correction explains why volatile investments underperform less volatile ones at the same arithmetic mean return.

whenWhy.title: Ito's lemma for option pricing (Black-Scholes PDE)
whenWhy.body: Black-Scholes derived their PDE using Ito's lemma on V(S,t), the option value. Given dS = ОјS dt + ПғS dW, apply Ito's lemma to V(S,t): dV = (вҲӮV/вҲӮt + ОјS вҲӮV/вҲӮS + ВҪПғВІSВІ вҲӮВІV/вҲӮSВІ) dt + ПғS вҲӮV/вҲӮS dW. Form a delta-hedged portfolio: О  = V - (вҲӮV/вҲӮS) Г— S. The dW terms cancel (this is the delta hedge). The portfolio is instantaneously riskless: dО  = r О  dt (must earn risk-free rate by no-arbitrage). This yields the Black-Scholes PDE: вҲӮV/вҲӮt + rS вҲӮV/вҲӮS + ВҪПғВІSВІ вҲӮВІV/вҲӮSВІ - rV = 0. Solved with boundary condition V(S,T) = max(S-K, 0) for a call, gives the Black-Scholes formula.

whenWhy.title: Ito's product rule and integration by parts
whenWhy.body: Ito's product rule: d(XY) = X dY + Y dX + dX dY. The extra term dX dY is the covariation вҖ” survives when X and Y are both driven by the same Brownian motion. For two processes: dX = a dt + b dW, dY = c dt + d dW (same W): d(XY) = X dY + Y dX + bd dt (the covariation). This is used in: (1) Stochastic integration by parts (useful for pricing claims on correlated assets); (2) Deriving dynamics of portfolio value (if portfolio has two risky assets driven by correlated Brownian motions); (3) Martingale representation вҖ” any martingale driven by Brownian motion can be written as a stochastic integral. Applications in fixed income: pricing of interest rate derivatives where the bond price and rate are both driven by Brownian motion requires Ito's product rule.

deepDiveIntro: Ito's lemma (one-dimensional, time-homogeneous): for f(X_t) where dX_t = a(X_t,t) dt + b(X_t,t) dW_t: df(X_t) = f'(X_t) dX_t + ВҪ f''(X_t)(dX_t)ВІ = [a f' + ВҪ bВІ f''] dt + b f' dW_t. The key: (dW)ВІ = dt (quadratic variation of Brownian motion), (dW)(dt) = 0, (dt)ВІ = 0. Two-dimensional Ito's lemma: for f(X_t, Y_t): df = вҲӮf/вҲӮx dX + вҲӮf/вҲӮy dY + ВҪ вҲӮВІf/вҲӮxВІ (dX)ВІ + вҲӮВІf/вҲӮxвҲӮy dX dY + ВҪ вҲӮВІf/вҲӮyВІ (dY)ВІ. General form (time-dependent): df(X_t, t) includes the вҲӮf/вҲӮt dt term (the 'theta' in options). This generalises to vector processes with correlated Brownian motions.

deepDive.heading: Quadratic Variation: Why (dW)ВІ = dt
deepDive.body: The quadratic variation of a process X_t is [X,X]_T = lim_{||О ||вҶ’0} ОЈ (X_{t_{i+1}} - X_{t_i})ВІ. For Brownian motion W_t, the quadratic variation is [W,W]_T = T (deterministic). Proof sketch: E[(О”W_i)ВІ] = О”t_i (by Brownian motion increments variance); summing over all intervals gives T. Variance of the sum converges to 0 as mesh size goes to 0. This means (dW)ВІ = dt in the Ito integral sense вҖ” not just on average, but almost surely (pathwise). Contrast: for a smooth function f(t), [f,f]_T = 0 (ordinary calculus). For Brownian motion: [W,W]_T = T вү  0. The non-zero quadratic variation is why Brownian motion is non-differentiable and why ordinary calculus fails. It's also why Ito integrals require the extra ВҪ bВІ f'' dt correction term. [1]

deepDive.heading: Ito vs. Stratonovich Integrals
deepDive.body: Two conventions for stochastic integrals: Ito integral: вҲ«вӮҖбөҖ f(t) dW_t uses left-endpoint evaluation (non-anticipating). Stratonovich integral: вҲ«вӮҖбөҖ f(t) вҲҳ dW_t uses midpoint evaluation. Key difference: Ito integral is a martingale (zero mean); Stratonovich integral obeys the ordinary chain rule but is not a martingale. For Ito: d(WВІ) = 2W dW + dt (the extra dt is the Ito correction). For Stratonovich: d(WВІ) = 2W вҲҳ dW (no correction, ordinary chain rule). Interpretation: Stratonovich integral is more natural for physics (where noise is physical and has correlations on short time scales); Ito integral is more natural for finance (non-anticipating: trading decisions at time t cannot depend on future prices). The connection: вҲ«f dW (Ito) = вҲ«f вҲҳ dW (Stratonovich) - ВҪ вҲ«f' dt. Finance universally uses Ito; physics often uses Stratonovich. [1]

deepDive.heading: Ito's Lemma and the Numeraire Change
deepDive.body: A numeraire is the asset used to express prices. Changing the numeraire changes the drift but not the diffusion of an asset price process. Girsanov's theorem: the change from physical measure (P, with drift Ој) to risk-neutral measure (Q, with drift r) is a change of numeraire (from money market account to the risk-neutral measure). Under Q: dS = r S dt + Пғ S dW^Q. Ito's lemma allows deriving the dynamics of S/N_t (price in units of numeraire N_t) under any numeraire. This is essential for pricing: (1) Bond numeraire: use the bond as numeraire for interest rate derivatives (T-forward measure); (2) Stock numeraire: use the stock as numeraire for pricing exchange options; (3) Swap measure: use the annuity as numeraire for swaption pricing. The numeraire-free pricing equation: V_t/N_t is a martingale under the measure associated with numeraire N_t. This unification simplifies exotic derivatives pricing. [2]

takeaways: Ito's lemma: df = f' dX + ВҪ f'' (dX)ВІ because (dW)ВІ = dt for Brownian motion (non-zero quadratic variation). [1]
Applied to ln(S): d(ln S) = (Ој - ПғВІ/2) dt + Пғ dW вҖ” the -ПғВІ/2 Ito correction is crucial for long-run returns. [1]
Black-Scholes PDE derived by applying Ito's lemma to V(S,t) and forming a delta-hedged portfolio. [1]
Ito integral is non-anticipating (left-endpoint); Stratonovich uses ordinary chain rule but is anticipating. [1]
Girsanov's theorem: change from P (drift Ој) to Q (drift r) for risk-neutral pricing is a numeraire change. [2]

edgeCases.q: What happens to Ito's lemma when the function is not twice differentiable?
edgeCases.a: Ito's lemma requires f to be twice continuously differentiable (CВІ in S). Problems arise for: (1) Option payoffs: max(S-K, 0) is not differentiable at S=K; the second derivative is a Dirac delta at K вҖ” but the integral is well-defined and Black-Scholes still works via the Feynman-Kac theorem; (2) Barrier options: payoffs that switch at barrier levels require careful treatment at the boundary (local time at the barrier contributes to the Ito correction via the Tanaka formula); (3) Lookback options: payoffs depend on running maximum max_{tвүӨT} S_t вҖ” handled via reflection principle of Brownian motion. The Tanaka-Meyer formula extends Ito to convex but non-smooth functions, making the local time at non-smooth points explicit.

edgeCases.q: How does Ito's lemma extend to jump processes?
edgeCases.a: For a jump-diffusion process (Merton model): dS = ОјS dt + ПғS dW + S dJ, where J is a compound Poisson process with jump sizes {Z_i}. Ito's lemma for jump processes: df = [a f' + ВҪ bВІ f''] dt + b f' dW + ОЈ [f(S + О”S_i) - f(S)] dJ_i. The extra jump term accounts for discrete jumps вҖ” the function value jumps discontinuously. For ln(S): d(ln S) = (Ој - ПғВІ/2) dt + Пғ dW + ln(1 + О”S/S) dJ. The jump contribution to the log-return is ln(1 + Z), not Z вҖ” important for fat-tail modelling. Merton (1976) derives a closed-form option pricing formula for this process as a sum of Black-Scholes formulas weighted by Poisson probabilities.

sources.label: Oksendal, B. вҖ” Stochastic Differential Equations (6th ed.), Springer
Shreve, S.E. вҖ” Stochastic Calculus for Finance II, Springer
